content: Two events A and B are such that P (not B) = 0.8, P (A $$\cup$$ B) = 0.5 and P (A / B) = 0.4. Then, P(A) is equal to
explanation:
Given, P($$\overline B $$) = 0.8

$$\Rightarrow$$ P(B) = 1 $$-$$ P($$\overline B $$) = 1 $$-$$ 0.8 = 0.2

P(A $$\cup$$ B) = 0.5, P(A / B) = 0.4

$$\therefore$$ P(A / B) = $${{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(B)}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ 0.4 = $${{P(A \cap B)} \over {0.2}}$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ 0.08 = P(A $$\cap$$ B)

Hence,

P(A $$\cup$$ B) = P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)

$$\Rightarrow$$ 0.5 = P(A) + 0.2 $$-$$ 0.08

$$\Rightarrow$$ 0.5 = P(A) + 0.12

$$\Rightarrow$$ P(A) = 0.38

content: If mean and variance of a binomial variate X are 2 and 1 respectively, then the probability that X takes a value greater than 1, is
explanation:
Given, np = 2 and npq = 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ 2 $$\times$$ q = 1 $$\Rightarrow$$ q = $${1 \over 2}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ p + q = 1 $$\Rightarrow$$ p = $${1 \over 2}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ n $$\times$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ = 1 $$\Rightarrow$$ n = 4

P(X > 1) = $${}^4{C_2}$$p2q2 + $${}^4{C_3}$$p3q1 + $${}^4{C_4}$$p4q0

= $$6{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2} + 4{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^3}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^1} + 1 \times {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^4}$$

$$ = {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^4}[6 + 4 + 1] = {{11} \over {16}}$$

n = 4, r = 4 and N = 5

= $${5 \over {16}}$$

content:

explanation:

Probability of the experiments end with three tosses if TTH comes

content: A bag contains 4 white and 2 black balls and another bag contains 3 white and 5 black balls. If one ball is drawn from each bag, then the probability that one ball is white and one ball is black, is
explanation:
P (one ball is white and one ball is black).

= P(black ball from bag 1 and white ball from bag 2 or white ball from bag 1 and black ball from bag 2)

= $${2 \over 6}$$ $$\times$$ $${3 \over 8}$$ + $${4 \over 6}$$ $$\times$$ $${5 \over 8}$$ = $${26 \over 48}$$ = $${13 \over 24}$$

content: A problem in Statistics is given to three students A, B and C whose chances of solving it independently are $${1 \over 2}$$, $${1 \over 3}$$ and $${1 \over 4}$$, respectively. The probability that the problem will be solved, is
explanation:
P (Problem will be solved) = 1 $$-$$ P

(Problem will not solved by A, B and C)

$$ = 1 - \left\{ {\left( {1 - {1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 4}} \right)} \right\}$$

$$ = 1 - {1 \over 2} \times {2 \over 3} \times {3 \over 4} = 1 - {1 \over 4} = {3 \over 4}$$

content: An insurance company insured 2000 scooter drivers, 4000 car drivers and 6000 truck drivers. The probabilities of an accident involving a scooter driver, car driver and a truck driver are 0.01, 0.03 and 0.15, respectively. One of the insured persons meets with an accident. The probability that the person is a scooter driver, is
explanation:
Let

P(A) = P(scooter) = $${{2000} \over {12000}} = {1 \over 6}$$

P(B) = P(car) = $${{4000} \over {12000}} = {1 \over 3}$$

P(C) = P(truck) = $${{6000} \over {12000}} = {1 \over 2}$$

Let E = Event that person meets with accident.

We have,

$$P\left( {{E \over A}} \right) = {1 \over {100}},P\left( {{E \over B}} \right) = {3 \over {100}},P\left( {{E \over C}} \right) = {{15} \over {100}}$$

Now,

$$P\left( {{A \over E}} \right) = {{P(A).P\left( {{E \over A}} \right)} \over {P(A).P\left( {{E \over A}} \right) + P(B).P\left( {{E \over B}} \right) + P(C).P\left( {{E \over C}} \right)}}$$

$$ = {{{1 \over 6} \times {1 \over {100}}} \over {{1 \over 6} \times {1 \over {100}} + {1 \over 3} \times {3 \over {100}} + {1 \over 2} \times {{15} \over {100}}}}$$

$$ = {{{1 \over 6}} \over {{1 \over 6} + 1 + {{15} \over 2}}} = {{{1 \over 6}} \over {{{1 + 6 + 45} \over 6}}} = {1 \over {52}}$$

content: A coin is tossed 5 times. The probability that tail appears an odd number of times, is
explanation:
We have,

p = Probability of tail appear = $${1 \over 2}$$

q = Probability of tail not appear = 1 $$-$$ p = $${1 \over 2}$$

Now, P (Tail appear odd number of times)

= P(X = 1) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 5) [$$\because$$ P(X = r)]

= $${}^n{C_r}{p^r}{q^{n - r}}$$

= $${}^5{C_1}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^1}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^4} + {}^5{C_3}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^3}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2} + {}^5{C_5}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^5}$$

$$ = {5 \over {32}} + {{10} \over {32}} + {1 \over {32}} = {{16} \over {32}} = {1 \over 2}$$

content: What is the probability that the sum of any two different single digit natural numbers is a prime number?
A special dice with numbers 1, $$-$$1, 2, $$-$$2, 0 and 3 is thrown thrice. What is the probability that the sum of the numbers occurring on the upper face is zero ?
explanation:
Let p, q and r be the numbers on the upper faces in first, second and third thrown respectively.

Now, number of favourable cases such that sum of upper faces is zero equals to number of integral solution of p + q + r = 0 subject to condition $$-$$2 $$\le$$ p, q, r $$\le$$ 3, i.e. equal to coefficient of t0 in (t$$-$$2 + t$$-$$1 + ... + t3)3

= coefficient of t6 in (1 + t + ... + t5)3

= coefficient of t6 in $${\left( {{{1 - {t^6}} \over {1 - t}}} \right)^3}$$

= coefficient of t6 in (1 $$-$$ t6)3(1 $$-$$ t)$$-$$3

= coefficient of t6 in (1 $$-$$ $${}^3{C_1}$$t6 + ...) (1 $$-$$ t)$$-$$3

= coefficient of t6 in (1 $$-$$ t)$$-$$3 $$-$$ $${}^3{C_1}$$ $$\times$$ coefficient of t0 in (1 $$-$$ t)$$-$$3

= $${}^8{C_2}$$ $$-$$ 3 = 25

$$\therefore$$ Required probability = $${{25} \over {216}}$$

explanation:
Given,

p = P(chance that it rains) = 25%

$$\Rightarrow$$ p = $${{25} \over {100}} = {1 \over 4}$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ q = 1 $$-$$ p = 1 $$-$$ $${1 \over 4}$$ = $${3 \over 4}$$

P (at least one rainy day within a period of 7 days) = 1 $$-$$ $${}^7{C_0}$$p0q7 = 1 $$-$$ $${\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right)^7}$$

content: A salesman has a 70% chance to sell a product to any customer. The behaviour of successive customers is independent. If two customers A and B enter, what is the probability that the salesman will sell the product to customer A or B ?
explanation:
Probability of salesman making a sale = $${{70} \over {100}} = 0.70$$

Let A and B be the events that salesman sell the product to A and B respectively.

$$\therefore$$ P(A) = 0.7 and P(B) = 0.7

Since, A and B are independent.

$$\therefore$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B) = P(A) . P(B) = 0.7 $$\times$$ 0.7 = 0.49

Now, P(A $$\cup$$ B) = P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)

= 0.7 + 0.7 $$-$$ 0.49 = 0.91

content: A student appears for tests I, II and III. The student is considered successful if he passes in tests I, II or I, III or all the three. The probabilities of the student passing in tests I, II and III are m, n and 1/2 respectively. If the probability of the student to be successful is 1/2, then which one of the following is correct?
explanation:
Let the events be

A = Passes in test I

B = Passes in test II

C = Passes in test III

Given,

P(A) = m, P($$\overline A $$) = 1 $$-$$ m

P(B) = n, P($$\overline B $$) = 1 $$-$$ n

P(C) = $${1 \over 2}$$, P($$\overline C $$) = $${1 \over 2}$$

Student is considered successful if he passes in test I, II or I, III or all the three.

$$\therefore$$ Required probability

= P(AB$$\overline C $$) + P(A$$\overline B $$C) + P(ABC)

= P(A) $$\times$$ P(B) $$\times$$ P($$\overline C $$) + P(A) $$\times$$ P($$\overline B $$) $$\times$$ P(C) + P(A) $$\times$$ P(B) $$\times$$ P(C)

= $${{mn} \over 2} + {{m(1 - n)} \over 2} + {{mn} \over 2}$$

$$ = {{mn + m - mn + mn} \over 2} = {{m(n + 1)} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = {{m(n + 1)} \over 2}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ m(1 + n) = 1

content: The candidates solve a question. Odds in favour of the correct answer are 5 : 2, 4 : 3, and 3 : 4 respectively for the three candidates. What is the probability that at least two of them solve the question correctly?
explanation:
Let the students be E1, E2 and E3 and P(E1), P(E2) and P(E3) denotes the probabilities of the students to solve question correctly.

$$\because$$ Odds in favour of the correct answer are 5 : 2, 4 : 3, 3 : 4, respectively.

$$\therefore$$ $$P({E_1}) = {5 \over {5 + 2}} = {5 \over 7}$$

$$P({E_2}) = {4 \over {4 + 3}} = {4 \over 7}$$

$$P({E_3}) = {3 \over {3 + 4}} = {3 \over 7}$$

$$\therefore$$ Probability that at least two students solve question correctly

$$ = P({E_1}{E_2}\overline {{E_3}} ) + P(\overline {{E_1}} {E_2}{E_3}) + P({E_1}\overline {{E_2}} {E_3}) + P({E_1}{E_2}{E_3})$$

$$ = P({E_1})P({E_2})P(\overline {{E_3}} ) + P(\overline {{E_1}} )P({E_2})P({E_3}) + P({E_1})P(\overline {{E_2}} )P({E_3}) + P({E_1})P({E_2})P({E_3})$$

$$ = {5 \over 7}\,.\,{4 \over 7}\left( {1 - {3 \over 7}} \right) + \left( {1 - {5 \over 7}} \right)\,.\,{4 \over 7}\,.\,{3 \over 7} + {5 \over 7}\left( {1 - {4 \over 7}} \right){3 \over 7} + {5 \over 7}\,.\,{4 \over 7}\,.\,{3 \over 7}$$

$$ = {5 \over 7}.{4 \over 7}.{4 \over 7} + {2 \over 7}.{4 \over 7}.{3 \over 7} + {5 \over 7}.{3 \over 7}.{3 \over 7} + {5 \over 7}.{4 \over 7}.{3 \over 7}$$

$$ = {1 \over {7 \times 7 \times 7}}(80 + 24 + 45 + 60)$$

$$ = {{209} \over {343}}$$

content: A medicine is known to be 75% effective to cure a patient. If the medicine is given to 5 patients, what is the probability that at least one patient is cured by this medicine?
explanation:
Let p[ denotes the probability that medicine to be 75% effective to cure a patient, then

$$p = 75\% = {{75} \over {100}} = {3 \over 4}$$

$$q = 1 - p = 1 - {3 \over 4} = {1 \over 4}$$

Let X denotes the probability that at least one patient is cured by the medicine

$$ = 1 - {}^5{C_0}{(p)^0}{(q)^5}$$

$$ = 1 - {}^5{C_0}{\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right)^0}{\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)^5}$$

$$ = 1 - {\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)^5} = 1 - {1 \over {1024}}$$

$$ = {{1023} \over {1024}}$$

content: For two events A and B, it is given that $$P(A) = {3 \over {5}}$$ $$P(B) = {3 \over {10}}$$ and $$P(A/B) = {2 \over 3}$$. If $$\overline A $$ and $$\overline B $$ are the complementary events of A and B, then what is $$P(\overline A /\overline B )$$ equal to ?
explanation:
Given, $$P(A) = {3 \over {5}}$$ $$P(B) = {3 \over {10}}$$ and $$P\left( {{A \over B}} \right) = {2 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(B)}} = {2 \over 3} \Rightarrow {{P(A \cap B)} \over {3/10}} = {2 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A \cap B) = {2 \over 3} \times {3 \over {10}} = {1 \over 5}$$

Now, $$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A \cup B) = {3 \over 5} + {3 \over {10}} - {1 \over 5} = {{6 + 3 - 2} \over {10}} = {7 \over {10}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(\overline {A \cup B} ) = 1 - P(A \cup B) = 1 - {7 \over {10}} = {3 \over {10}}$$ and

$$P(\overline B ) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - {3 \over {10}} = {7 \over {10}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$P(\overline A /\overline B ) = {{P(\overline A \cap \overline B )} \over {P(\overline B )}} = {{P(\overline {A \cup B} )} \over {P(\overline B )}}$$

$$ = {{3/10} \over {7/10}} = {3 \over 7}$$

content: A machine had three parts, A, B and C, whose chances of being defective are 0.02, 0.10 and 0.05 respectively. The machine stops working if any one of the parts becomes defective. What is the probability that the machine will not stop working?
explanation:
Given,

$$P(A) = 0.02 = {2 \over {100}} = {1 \over {50}}$$

$$P(B) = 0.10 = {{10} \over {100}} = {1 \over {10}}$$ and

$$P(C) = 0.05 = {5 \over {100}} = {1 \over {20}}$$

Probability of the machine will stop, if at least one of its three components fails

$$ = P(A \cup B \cup C) = 1 - P(\overline {A \cup B \cup C} )$$

$$ = 1 - P(\overline A \cap \overline B \cap \overline C )$$

$$ = 1 - P(\overline A )P(\overline B )P(\overline C )$$

$$ = 1 - \left( {1 - {1 \over {50}}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over {10}}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over {20}}} \right)$$

$$ = 1 - \left( {{{49} \over {50}}} \right)\left( {{9 \over {10}}} \right)\left( {{{19} \over {20}}} \right) = 1 - {{8379} \over {10000}}$$

$$ = 1 - 0.8379 = 0.1621$$

Now, required probability of that the machine will not stop working

$$ = 1 - 0.1621 = 0.8379 = 0.84$$

content: Three independent events, A<sub>1</sub>, A<sub>2</sub> and A<sub>3</sub> occur with probabilities $$P({A_i}) = {1 \over {1 + i}}$$, i = 1, 2, 3. What is the probability that at least one of the three events occurs?
explanation:
Given, A1, A2 and A3 are independent events such that $$P({A_i}) = {1 \over {1 + i}}$$, where i = 1, 2, 3

The probability that at least one of three events occurs

$$ = P({A_1} \cup {A_2} \cup {A_3})$$

$$ = 1 - P({\overline A _1})P({\overline A _2})P({\overline A _3})$$

$$ = 1 - \left( {1 - {1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 4}} \right)$$

$$ = 1 - \left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right) = 1 - {1 \over 4} = {3 \over 4}$$

content:
A coin is tossed three times. Consider the following events.

A : No head appears.

B : Exactly one head appears.

C : At least two heads appear.

Which one of the following is correct?
explanation:
If a coin is tossed three times, then

S = {HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT}

A = No head appears = {TTT}

B = Exactly one head appears = {TTH, THT, HTT}

C = At least two head appear = {HHH, HHT, HTH, THH}

Clearly, B $$\cap$$ C = $$\phi$$

Now, A $$\cap$$ (B' $$\cup$$ C') = A $$\cap$$ (B $$\cap$$ C)'

= A $$\cap$$ ($$\phi$$)' [$$\because$$ B $$\cap$$ C = $$\phi$$]

= A $$\cap$$ S = A

and B' $$\cap$$ C' = (B $$\cup$$ C)' = A

Hence, A $$\cap$$ (B' $$\cup$$ C') = B' $$\cap$$ C'

content: In a series of 3 one-day cricket matches between teams A and B of a college, the probability of team A winning or drawing are 1/3 and 1/6 respectively. If a win, loss or draw gives 2, 0 and 1 point respectively, then what is the probability that team A will score 5 points in the series ?
explanation:
We have, P(winning) = $${{1} \over {3}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ P(drawing) = $${{1} \over {6}}$$

P(loss) = 1 $$-$$ $$\left( {{1 \over 3} + {1 \over 6}} \right) = {1 \over 2}$$

Point of win, loss or draw are 2, 0, 1 respectively, 5 points are possible, in three cases (2, 2, 1) (2, 1, 2) (1, 2, 2)

$$\therefore$$ Required probability

= P(WWD) + P(WDW) + P(DWW)

= 3 $$\times$$ $${1 \over 3}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 3}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 6}$$ = $${1 \over 18}$$

content: Let the random variable X follow B (6, p). If 16 P(X = 4) = P (X = 2), then what is the value of p?
explanation:
Given, binomial distribution B(6, p).

Here, n = 6

According to the question,

16P (X = 4) = P(X = 2)

$$\Rightarrow$$ $$16{}^6{C_4}{p^4}{q^2} = {}^6{C_2}{p^2}{q^4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{}^6{C_2}{p^4}{q^2} = {}^6{C_2}{p^2}{q^4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{p^2} = {q^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{p^2} = {(1 - p)^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{p^2} = 1 + {p^2} - 2p$$

$$ \Rightarrow 15{p^2} + 2p - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 15{p^2} + 5p - 3p - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 5p(3p + 1) - 1(3p + 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (3p + 1)(5p - 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3p + 1 = 0$$ or $$5p - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow p = - {1 \over 3}$$ or $$p = {1 \over 5}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$p = {1 \over 5}$$, since $$p = - {1 \over 3}$$ is not possible.

content: A fair coin is tossed 100 times. What is the probability of getting tails an odd number of times ?
explanation:
Clearly, total number of outcomes = 2100 and number of favourable outcomes

$$ = {}^{100}{C_1} + {}^{100}{C_3} + .... + {}^{100}{C_{99}} = {2^{100 - 1}}$$

[$$\because$$ $${}^n{C_1} + {}^n{C_3} + {}^n{C_5} + ... = {2^{n - 1}}$$]

$$ = {2^{99}}$$

Hence, the required probability

$$ = {{{2^{99}}} \over {{2^{100}}}} = {1 \over 2}$$

content: Three dice are thrown simultaneously. What is the probability that the sum on the three faces is at least 5 ?
Two independent events A and B have P(A) = $${1 \over 3}$$ and P(B) = $${3 \over 4}$$. What is the probability that exactly one of the two events A or B occurs?
explanation:
Given, P(A) = $${1 \over 3}$$ and P(B) = $${3 \over 4}$$

Now, P(exactly one)

= P(A $$\cup$$ B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)

= P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)

[$$\because$$ P(A $$\cup$$ B) = P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)]

= P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ 2(P(A $$\cap$$ B))

= P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ 2P(A) . P(B) [$$\because$$ event, A and B are independent]

$$ = {1 \over 3} + {3 \over 4} - 2.{1 \over 3}.{3 \over 4}$$

$$ = {1 \over 3} + {3 \over 4} - {1 \over 2}$$

$$ = {{4 + 9 - 6} \over {12}} = {7 \over {12}}$$

content: A coin is tossed three times. What is the probability of getting head and tail alternately?
explanation:
Given, a coin is tossed three times.

Now, required probability

= P(HTH) + P(THT)

= $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ + $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ [$$\because$$ P(H) = P(T) = $${1 \over 2}$$]

= $${1 \over 8}$$ + $${1 \over 8}$$ = $${2 \over 8}$$ = $${1 \over 4}$$

content: A card is drawn from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. What is the probability that it is queen of spade?
explanation:
A card can be chosen from a pack of 52 cards in $${}^{52}{C_1}$$ ways = 52 ways.

Queen of space can be chosen from pack in only 1 way.

So, probability of choosing queen of spade = $${1 \over {52}}$$.

content: If two dice are thrown, then what is the probability that the sum on the two faces is greater than or equal to 4?
explanation:
For 2 dice, possible outcomes are, (1, 1), (1, 2), ......, (6, 6)

A total of 36 possible outcomes out of these 36 outcomes, only 3 outcomes (1, 1), (2, 1) and (1, 2) are non-favourable and remaining 33 are favourable (sum $$\ge$$ 4).

So, probability of getting a sum $$\ge$$ 4

$$ = {{33} \over {36}} = {{11} \over {12}}$$

content: A certain type of missile hits the target with probability p = 0.3. What is the least number of missiles should be fired, so that there is at least an 80% probability that the target is hit?
explanation:
Let n missiles be fired and r of them hits the target.

$$\therefore$$ P(X = r) = $${}^n{C_r}$$prqn $$-$$ r

= $${}^n{C_r}$$ (0.3)r (0.7)n $$-$$ r

Target is hit when at least 1 missile strikes the target.

$$\therefore$$ P(X $$\ge$$ 1) = 1 $$-$$ P(X = 0)

This must be greater than 80%

So, 1 $$-$$ $${}^n{C_0}$$(0.3)0 (0.7)n $$-$$ 0 $$\ge$$ $${{80} \over {100}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ $$1 - {\left( {{7 \over {10}}} \right)^n} \ge {{80} \over {100}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{7 \over {10}}} \right)^n} \le {{20} \over {100}}$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ n $$\ge$$ 5

content: For two mutually exclusive events A and B, P(A) = 0.2 and P($$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ $$\overline B $$) = 0.3. What is P(A / (A $$\cup$$ B)) equal to ?
explanation:
As, $$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ B = B $$-$$ A $$\cap$$ B

So, in given case, P($$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ B) = P(B) = 0.3 [$$\because$$ A and B are mutually exclusive, so A $$\cap$$ B = $$\phi$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B) = 0]

and P(A / A $$\cup$$ B) = $${{P[A \cap (A \cup B)]} \over {P(A \cup B)}}$$

$$ = {{P(A)} \over {P(A) + P(B)}} = {{0.2} \over {0.2 + 0.3}} = {2 \over 5}$$

content: What is the probability of 5 Sunday in the month of December ?
explanation:
Number of days in December = 31

$$\therefore$$ Number of complete weeks = 4 (i.e. 7 $$\times$$ 4 = 28 days)

Remaining 3 days can be

(M, T, W), (T, W, Th), (W, Th, F), (Th, F, Sa), (F, Sa, S), (Sa, S, M), (S, M, T)

Out of these 7, 3 are favourable outcomes.

So, probability of having 5 Sunday in the month of December is $${3 \over 7}$$.

content: A point is chosen at random inside a rectangle measuring 6 inches by 5 inches. What is the probability that the randomly selected point is at least one inch from the edge of the rectangle?
explanation:
Let A2 = Area in which randomly selected point lies

content: A committee of two persons is selected from two men and two women. The probability that the committee will have exactly one woman is
explanation:
Let S be the event of selecting two persons from 2 men and 2 women.

$$\therefore$$ n(s) = $${}^4{C_2}$$

Let E be the event that selected person has 1 woman.

$$\therefore$$ n(E) = $${}^2{C_1} \times {}^2{C_1}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$P(E) = {{n(E)} \over {n(s)}} = {{{}^2{C_1} \times {}^2{C_1}} \over {{}^4{C_2}}} = {{2 \times 2} \over {\left( {{{4 \times 3} \over 2}} \right)}} = {2 \over 3}$$

content: Let a die be loaded in such a way that even faces are twice likely to occur as the odd faces. What is the probability that a prime number will show up when the die is tossed?
explanation:
Let the probability of number to be even and odd be p and q respectively.

$$\therefore$$ p + q = 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ 2q + q = 1 [$$\because$$ p(even) = 2p(odd)]

$$\Rightarrow$$ q = $${1 \over 3}$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ p = $${2 \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Probability of each even number

= $${1 \over 3}$$ $$\times$$ $${2 \over 3}$$ = $${2 \over 9}$$

and probability of each odd number

= $${1 \over 3}$$ $$\times$$ $${1 \over 3}$$ = $${1 \over 9}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required probability = Probability of prime number

= Probability of getting 2, 3, 5

= $${2 \over 9}$$ + $${1 \over 9}$$ + $${1 \over 9}$$ = $${4 \over 9}$$

content:

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
explanation:

$$\Rightarrow$$ n(s) = 51

X = Number which are multiple of 3

= {0, 3, 6, 9, ......., 48}

$$\Rightarrow$$ n(X) = 17

Y = Numbers which are odd

= {1, 3, 5, ......, 49}

$$\Rightarrow$$ n(Y) = 25

$$\therefore$$ $$P(X) = {{n(X)} \over {n(s)}} = {{17} \over {51}} = {1 \over 3}$$

and $$P(Y) = {{n(Y)} \over {n(s)}} = {{25} \over {51}}$$

content: For two events A and B, let $$P(A) = {1 \over 2}$$, $$P(A \cup B) = {2 \over 3}$$ and $$P(A \cap B) = {1 \over 6}$$. What is $$P(\overline A  \cap B)$$ equal to ?
explanation:
We have,

$$P(A) = {1 \over 2},P(A \cup B) = {2 \over 3},P(A \cap B) = {1 \over 6}$$

We know that,

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$${2 \over 3} = {1 \over 2} + P(B) - {1 \over 6} \Rightarrow P(B) = {1 \over 3}$$

Again, $$P(\overline A \cap B) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$$ = {1 \over 3} - {1 \over 6} = {1 \over 6}$$

content: Let A and B be two events with $$P(A) = {1 \over 3}$$, $$P(B) = {1 \over 6}$$ and $$P(A \cap B) = {1 \over {12}}$$. What is $$P(B\,|\,\overline A )$$ equal to ?
explanation:
We have, $$P(A) = {1 \over 3}$$, $$P(B) = {1 \over 6},P(A \cap B) = {1 \over {12}}$$

Now, $$P(B/\overline A ) = {{P(B \cap \overline A )} \over {P(\overline A )}} = {{P(B) - P(A \cap B)} \over {1 - P(A)}}$$

[$$\because$$ $$P(B \cap \overline A ) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$ and $$P(\overline A ) = 1 - P(A)$$]

$$ = {{{1 \over 6} - {1 \over {12}}} \over {1 - {1 \over 3}}} = {{{1 \over {12}}} \over {{2 \over 3}}} = {1 \over 8}$$

content: In a binomial distribution, the mean is $${2 \over 3}$$ and the variance is $${5 \over 9}$$. What is the probability that X = 2 ?
The probability that a ship safely reaches a port is $${1 \over 3}$$. The probability that out of 5 ships, at least 4 ships would arrive safely is
explanation:
According to the question,

n = 5, p = $${1 \over {3}}$$, q = 1 $$-$$ P = $${2 \over {3}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required probability = P(x $$\ge$$ 4)

= P(x = 4) + P(x = 5)

= $${}^5{C_4}{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^4}{\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^1} + {}^5{C_5}{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^5}{\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^0}$$

$$ = {{5 \times 2} \over {{3^5}}} + {{1 \times 1} \over {{3^5}}} = {{10} \over {243}} + {1 \over {243}} = {{11} \over {243}}$$

content: What is the probability that at least two persons out of a group of three persons were born in the same month (disregard year) ?
explanation:
P (None born in same month)

$$ = {{12} \over {12}} \times {{11} \over {12}} \times {{10} \over {12}} = {{110} \over {144}}$$

$$\therefore$$ P (at least two person born in same month)

= 1 $$-$$ P (none born in same month)

$$ = 1 - {{110} \over {144}} = {{144 - 110} \over {144}} = {{34} \over {144}} = {{17} \over {72}}$$

content: If $$P(B) = {3 \over 4}$$, $$P(A \cap B \cap \overline C ) = {1 \over 3}$$ and $$P(\overline A  \cap B \cap \overline C ) = {1 \over 3}$$, then what is $$P(B \cap C)$$ equal to ?
explanation:
We know that,

$$P(B \cap \overline C ) = P(A \cap B \cap \overline C ) + P(\overline A \cap B \cap \overline C )$$

$$ = {1 \over 3} + {1 \over 3} = {2 \over 3}$$ (given)

Also, $$P(B) = P(B \cap \overline C ) + P(B \cap \overline C )$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3 \over 4} = P(B \cap C) + {2 \over 3}$$ [$$\because$$ $$P(B) = {3 \over 4}$$ and $$P(B \cap \overline C ) = {2 \over 3}$$]

$$ \Rightarrow P(B \cap C) = {3 \over 4} - {2 \over 3} = {1 \over {12}}$$

content: In a multiple-choice test, an examinee either knows the correct answer with probability p, or guesses with probability 1 $$-$$ p. The probability of answering a question correctly is $${1 \over m}$$, if he or she merely guesses. If the examinee answers a question correctly, the probability that he or she really knows the answer is
explanation:
Let E1 be the event that examinee knows the answer.

E2 be the event the examine guesses the answer.

A be the event that the answer is correct.

Now, according to the question

P(E1) = p

P(E2) = 1 $$-$$ p

P(A / E1) = 1

P(A / E2) = $${1 \over m}$$

Required probability = P(E1 / A)

$$={{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})} \over {P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}}$$

$$ = {{p \times 1} \over {p \times 1 + (1 - p) \times {1 \over m}}} = {{mp} \over {pm + 1 - p}}$$

$$ = {{mp} \over {1 + (m - 1)p}}$$

content: Five sticks of length 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9 feet are given. Three of these sticks are selected at random. What is the probability that the selected sticks can form a triangle?
explanation:

E be the event that selected sticks forms a triangle

n(E) = $${}^5{C_3}$$ $$-$$ 1 = 3

$$\therefore$$ $${P(E) = {{n(E)} \over {n(S)}} = {3 \over {10}} = 0.3}$$

content: A committee of two persons is constituted from two men and two women. What is the probability that the committee will have only women?
explanation:
Number of ways of forming a committee of two persons from two men and two women = $${}^4{C_2}$$

and number of ways of forming a committee of two persons from two men and two women in which only women are there = $${}^2{C_0}$$ $$\times$$ $${}^2{C_2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required probability $$ = {{{}^2{C_0} \times {}^2{C_2}} \over {{}^4{C_2}}}$$

$$ = {{1 \times 1} \over 6} = {1 \over 6}$$

content: A question is given to three students A, B and C whose chances of solving it are $${1 \over 2}$$, $${1 \over 3}$$ and $${1 \over 4}$$ respectively. What is the probability that the question will be solved?
explanation:
We have chances of solving the questions A, B and C are $${1 \over 2}$$, $${1 \over 3}$$ and $${1 \over 4}$$ respectively

$$\therefore$$ P(A) = $${1 \over 2}$$, P(B) = $${1 \over 3}$$, P(C) = $${1 \over 4}$$

P(A') = $${1 \over 2}$$, P(B') = $${2 \over 3}$$, P(C') = $${3 \over 4}$$

Required probability

= 1 $$-$$ probability of problem not solved

= 1 $$-$$ P(A') $$\times$$ P(B') $$\times$$ P(C')

= 1 $$-$$ $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${2 \over 3}$$ $$\times$$ $${3 \over 4}$$ = 1 $$-$$ $${1 \over 4}$$ = $${3 \over 4}$$

content: For two dependent events A and B, it is given that P(A) = 0.2 and P(B) = 0.5. If A $$ \subseteq $$ B, then the values of conditional probabilities P(A / B) and P(B / A) are respectively
explanation:
We have,

P(A) = 0.2, P(B) = 0.5

A $$\subseteq$$ B

$$\therefore$$ A $$\cap$$ B = A

$$\Rightarrow$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B) = P(A)

P(A / B) = $${{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(B)}}$$

$$ = {{P(A)} \over {P(B)}} = {{0.2} \over {0.5}} = {2 \over 5}$$

P(B / A) = $${{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(A)}} = {{P(A)} \over {P(A)}} = 1$$

content: A card is drawn from a well-shuffled ordinary deck of 52 cards. What is the probability that it is an ace?
explanation:

content:

Which of the above statement is/are correct?
explanation:

two events are mutually exclusive if the occurrence of one event prevents the occurrence of the other and the probability of the union of two mutually exclusive events is the sum of their individual probabilities.

content: If two fair dice are thrown, then what is the probability that the sum is neither 8 nor 9 ?
explanation:
Total number of outcomes when two fair dice are thrown = 6 $$\times$$ 6 = 36

Outcome in which sum is either 8 no 9 will be (2, 6), (3, 5), (3, 6), (4, 4), (4, 5), (5, 3), (5, 4), (6, 2), (6, 3).

$$\therefore$$ Total number of outcome after throwing two dice in which sum is either 8 or 9 = 9

$$\therefore$$ Probability that the sum is either 8 no 9 = $${9 \over {36}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Probability that the sum is neither 8 nor 9

$$ = 1 - {9 \over {36}} = {{27} \over {36}} = {3 \over 4}$$

content: Let A and B are two mutually exclusive events with $$P(A) = {1 \over 3}$$ and $$P(B) = {1 \over 4}$$. What is the value of $$P(\overline A  \cap \overline B )$$ ?
explanation:
Given, $$P(A) = {1 \over 3}$$ and $$P(B) = {1 \over 4}$$

$$\because$$ A and B are two mutually exclusive events

$$\therefore$$ $$P(A \cap B) = 0$$

We know,

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$$ = {1 \over 3} + {1 \over 4} - 0 = {7 \over {12}}$$

Now,

$$\therefore$$ $$P(\overline A \cap \overline B ) = P(\overline {A \cup B} )$$ [By De-Morgan's theorem]

$$ = 1 - P(A \cup B) = 1 - {7 \over {12}} = {5 \over {12}}$$

content: The mean and standard deviation of a binomial distribution are 12 and 2 respectively. What is the number of trials ?
explanation:
$$\because$$ Mean = 12 and standard deviation = 2

then, np = 12 and npq = 4

$$\Rightarrow$$ 12 $$\times$$ q = 4 $$\Rightarrow$$ q = $${1 \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ p = 1 $$-$$ q = 1 $$-$$ $${1 \over 3}$$ = $${2 \over 3}$$

and also, np = 12

$$\Rightarrow$$ n $$\times$$ $${2 \over 3}$$ = 12

$$\Rightarrow$$ n = 12 $$\times$$ $${3 \over 2}$$ = 18

Let A, B and C be three mutually exclusive and exhaustive events associated with a random experiment. If P(B) = 1.5 P(A) and P(C) = 0.5 P(B), then P(A) is equal to
explanation:
We have, P(B) = 1.5 P(A) = $${3 \over 2}$$ P(A)

and P(C) = 0.5 P(B) = $${1 \over 2}$$ P(B)

= $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ $${3 \over 2}$$ P(A) = $${3 \over 4}$$ P(A)

Now, A, B and C are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events

So, P(A) + P(B) + P(C) = 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ P(A) $$\left( {{{13} \over 4}} \right)$$ = 1 $$\Rightarrow$$ P(A) = $${{4 \over {13}}}$$

content: In a bolt factory, machines X, Y, Z manufacture bolts that are respectively 25%, 35% and 40% of the factory's total output. The machines X, Y, Z respectively produce 2%, 4% and 5% defective bolts. A bolt is drawn at random from the product and is found to be defective. What is the probability that it was manufactured by machine X?
explanation:
Let

A : bolt manufactured from machine X

B : bolt manufactured from machine Y

C : bolt manufactured from machine Z

and E : bolt is defective

$$\therefore$$ P(A) = 25% = $${25 \over {100}}$$

P(B) = 35% = $${35 \over {100}}$$

P(C) = 40% = $${40 \over {100}}$$

and P$$\left( {{E \over A}} \right)$$ = 2% = $${2 \over {100}}$$

P$$\left( {{E \over B}} \right)$$ = 4% = $${4 \over {100}}$$

P$$\left( {{E \over C}} \right)$$ = 5% = $${5 \over {100}}$$

Probability of defective bulb that was manufactured by machine X,

$$P\left( {{A \over E}} \right) = {{P(A)\,.\,P\left( {{E \over A}} \right)} \over {P(A)\,.\,P\left( {{E \over A}} \right) + P(B)\,.\,P\left( {{E \over B}} \right) + P(C)\,.\,P\left( {{E \over C}} \right)}}$$

$$ = {{{{25} \over {100}} \times {2 \over {100}}} \over {{{25} \over {100}} \times {2 \over {100}} + {{35} \over {100}} \times {4 \over {100}} + {{40} \over {100}} \times {5 \over {100}}}}$$

$$ = {{25 \times 2} \over {25 \times 2 + 35 \times 4 + 40 \times 5}}$$

$$ = {{50} \over {50 + 140 + 200}} = {{50} \over {390}} = {5 \over {39}}$$

content: 8 coins are tossed simultaneously. The probability of getting at least 6 heads is
explanation:

Probability of getting at least 6 heads.

content:
If probability of simultaneous occurrence of two events A and B is p and the probability that exactly one of A, B occurs is q, then which of the following is/are correct?

2. P($$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ $$\overline B $$) = 1 $$-$$ p $$-$$ q

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
explanation:
Given that,

P(A $$\cap$$ B) = P and

P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ 2P(A $$\cap$$ B) = q

Now, Statement 1

Given,

P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ 2P(A $$\cap$$ B) = q

$$\Rightarrow$$ 1 $$-$$ P($$\overline A $$) + 1 $$-$$ P($$\overline B $$) $$-$$ 2p = q

$$\Rightarrow$$ P($$\overline A $$) + P($$\overline B $$) = 2 $$-$$ 2p $$-$$ q

Statement 1 is correct.

For Statement 2

P($$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ $$\overline B $$) = 1 $$-$$ P(A $$\cup$$ B)

= 1 $$-$$ [P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)]

= 1 $$-$$ [P(A) + P(B) $$-$$ 2P(A $$\cap$$ B) + P(A $$\cap$$ B)]

= 1 $$-$$ [q + p] = 1 $$-$$ q $$-$$ p

Statement 2 is also correct.

content: In a binomial distribution, the mean is three times its variance. What is the probability of exactly 3 successes out of 5 trials ?
explanation:
According to the question,

Mean = 3 (Variance)

$$\Rightarrow$$ np = 3npq [where n = number of trials]

$$\Rightarrow$$ q = $${1 \over 3}$$

$$\because$$ p + q = 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ p + $${1 \over 3}$$ = 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ p = $${2 \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ p(X = 3) $$ = {}^5{C_3}{\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^3} \times {\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {{5!} \over {3!2!}} \times {\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^3} \times {\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^2} = {{80} \over {243}}$$

content:
Consider the following statements

I. P($$\overline A $$ $$\cup$$ B) = P($$\overline A $$) + P(B) $$-$$ P($$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ B)

II. P(A $$\cap$$ $$\overline B $$) = P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B)

III. P(A $$\cap$$ B) = P(B) P(A / B)

Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Here,

Statement I

P($$\overline A $$ $$\cup$$ B) = P($$\overline A $$) + P(B) $$-$$ P($$\overline A $$ $$\cap$$ B) is correct.

Statement II

P(A $$\cap$$ $$\overline B $$) = P(B) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B) is wrong as P(A $$\cap$$ $$\overline B $$) = P(A) $$-$$ P(A $$\cap$$ B).

Statement III

P(A $$\cap$$ B) = P(B) $$\times$$ P$$\left( {{A \over B}} \right)$$ is correct. [by conditional theorem]

Hence, Statements I and III are correct.

content: If A and B are two events such that 2P(A) = 3P(B), where 0 < P(A) < P(B) < 1, then which one of the following is correct?

options.content: P(A | B) < P(B | A) < P(A $$\cap$$ B)
P(A $$\cap$$ B) < P(B | A) < P(A | B)
P(B | A) < P(A | B) < P(A $$\cap$$ B)
P(A $$\cap$$ B) < P(A | B) < P(B | A)

explanation:
Given that, 2P(A) = 3P(B)

$$ \Rightarrow 2{{P(A)} \over {P(A \cap B)}} = {{3P(B)} \over {P(A \cap B)}}$$ [dividing both sides by P(A $$\cup$$ B)]

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} \times {{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(A)}} = {1 \over 3} \times {{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(B)}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} \times P\left( {{B \over A}} \right) = {1 \over 3}P\left( {{A \over B}} \right)$$
